dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for respiratory distress at the address in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex from the rescue squad. What prompted you to call 911 tonight?
I've been having trouble breathing and a swollen, painful lower area for about two weeks. It just got worse tonight.
Mr. Frank, can you open your eyes and respond to me now? What is your name again?

I'm Frank. I can hear you just fine. It's a 10 right now, feels like it's right up in my stomach too, though I don't have chest pain per se. I just can't stop panting. The swelling is bad in my legs, stomach, and testicles-it's all really sore.
Mr. Frank, can you move each arm and leg for me? Just squeeze my finger on each side if you can.
Your airway is patent and maintainable. You're in a recumbent position, which seems comfortable. Any unusual sounds or difficulty holding it open?
Mr. Frank, I'm going to listen to your lungs. Can you just breathe normally?
Slight expiratory wheezing is noted, and sounds are diminished in the bases-possibly due to body habitus.
You're breathing rapidly and shallow. That's tachypnea, and I notice increased effort in your chest movements. Is that normal for you?
Your pulse is strong, and your skin is warm and dry. There's swelling in the lower extremities that we'll note and recheck.
Radial pulses on both sides are strong and equal. Your skin is normal in appearance, but we'll keep monitoring the swelling.
When did the trouble breathing and the swelling begin?
The swelling in my feet started slowly over a couple of weeks and got worse. The testicle area began to hurt and swell. Today, it just felt like it went from bad to worse.
You mentioned pain in your testicles that seems constant. Is it sharp or burning in nature?
I had some toast with peanut butter maybe three hours ago. I've been too out of breath to eat much since then.
What chronic medical conditions do you have listed?
I have COPD, hypertension, heart failure, and obesity.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take albuterol, lisinopril, Lasix, allopurinol, lurasidone, atorvastatin, isosorbide mononitrate, gabapentin, hydralazine, carvedilol, ezetimibe, and methocarbamol.
Thank you. You also take hydroxyzine?
I observe swelling in both legs, mild pallor, and no signs of tearing or abrasions. The skin looks normal otherwise.
Pupils are equal and reactive. No stiff neck or head trauma noted.
I'm checking your mouth and throat. Any issues with your gag reflex or visible sores?
Breathing is shallow and labored. No obvious asymmetry in chest movement. Work of breathing is increased.
Your abdomen is slightly distended but soft. I'll avoid palpating it to keep you comfortable.
Let me check your legs and feet again. Swelling is notable from the knees down. No deformity, but edema is present. Are there any limitations with use of these limbs?
Pulse is 91, same as earlier readings.
Respiration is 23 per minute. It is quite shallow.
Blood pressure is 185 over 101 currently. That is elevated.
Oxygen saturation reads 96%-that is better than the earlier reading.
Blood glucose is not available at this reading, no glucometer readings taken per documented notes.
We'll perform another 4-Lead EKG now.
4-Lead EKG monitoring is now complete.
12-Lead EKG was completed. It shows sinus rhythm without ectopy and no acute findings.
Per protocol, VCU Hospital is the destination. All vitals are compatible for transport.
Transport is ready-need to secure you now. Our shift ends, and the ER is expecting your report. Preparing for stretcher movement.
Let me move myself-I want to help place myself on the stretcher.
You're now loaded and secured. All movement is stable and monitored.
Second set of vitals: pulse-90 respiration-31. Some increase from last check.
Oxygen saturation is now 89%.
Blood pressure is 198 over 122-upward trend.
Pain level up to a 10?
Still a 10. Sharp in the pelvic area and shoots up to the lower abdomen with movement.
Has the pain moved to a new location?
Still the same pain path-up through the groin and into the lower belly. No chest discomfort.
We're almost at the ER. Any final message or update for the hospital team?
All movement is smooth and secure. We are en route.
I'm doing fine-just try to keep taking deep breaths.
Latest vitals: pulse-88. Down slightly from earlier.
We've arrived at the VCU ER entrance now. All clear for handoff.
For the nurses, we have a 58-year-old male with COPD, hypertension, CHF, and obesity.
Lasix was increased from 40 mg to 60 mg due to his reported symptoms.
Final vitals: 88 pulse, 26 respiration, 192/130 BP, 95% oxygen.
Also, no acute findings on the 12-lead EKG.
Medications include Albuterol, lisinopril, Lasix, allopurinol, atorvastatin, isosorbide mononitrate, gabapentin, hydralazine, carvedilol, ezetimibe, lurasidone, hydroxyzine, and methocarbamol.
Thank you. We're ready to receive him.
Mr. Frank is now on the ER bed. No signs of change during transport.
No progression or changes noted in vitals or mental state.
We've passed all information and equipment to the hospital staff. Transport is complete.
Final check-same readings. Vitals at 88 and 95%, 26 respiration.
No change in the patient since transport ended. He still moved himself over to the hospital bed.
